home.heroTitle: Struggling with brain fog, fatigue, or weight that won't budge? {highlight}Find what YOUR body actually needs.{/highlight}
home.heroSubtitle: Struggling with brain fog, afternoon fatigue, or unexplained weight gain — even though you exercise regularly? Your one-size-fits-all diet might be the problem. TCM Wellness uses 2,000 years of Traditional Chinese Medicine wisdom — powered by AI — to give you personalized food, exercise, and lifestyle recommendations based on YOUR unique body type.

about.heroTitle: Are you struggling with brain fog, afternoon fatigue, and unexplained weight gain?
about.heroText: You've tried every diet, every wellness trend — but the results are always temporary. What if the answer isn't a new diet, but an ancient wisdom that sees your body as unique?

stage1Items.title: Brain Fog
stage1Items.desc: Can't focus by 2 PM? Your digestion might be pulling energy from your brain.

stage1Items.title: Afternoon Fatigue
stage1Items.desc: Feeling drained after lunch? Your body is struggling to process what you ate.

stage1Items.title: Weight Struggles
stage1Items.desc: Every diet works for others but not for you? Your body type needs a different approach.

about.stage2Label: Stage 2 · Why Western Methods Fall Short
about.stage2Title: Tried every method but results are temporary?
about.stage2Text1: Modern wellness treats symptoms, not root causes. It prescribes the same solution for everyone — counting calories, cutting carbs, eating less — without asking the most important question:
about.stage2Text2: Western nutrition sees food as numbers. TCM sees food as energy — warming or cooling, nourishing or draining — that interacts differently with every unique body.
about.stage3Title: Let's try 2,000 years of accumulated Chinese wisdom — for your body, uniquely.
about.stage3Text1: TCM (Traditional Chinese Medicine) has been personalized medicine for over two millennia. Its core insight is simple but profound: every person has a unique body constitution, and what heals one person may harm another.
about.stage3Text2: Our AI-powered platform brings this ancient wisdom to your fingertips:

stage3Items.text: Discover your unique TCM body type in 2 minutes
Scan any food to see if it's right for YOUR body
Get personalized meal, exercise, and lifestyle plans

about.ctaText: 2,000 years of wisdom. 2 minutes of your time. A lifetime of better wellness.

daily.title: Daily Wellness
daily.subtitle: Your daily dose of TCM wisdom
tips: Start your morning with a glass of warm water with lemon to gently awaken your digestive system.
Try eating your largest meal at lunch when your digestive fire (Spleen Qi) is strongest, between 11am-1pm.
Practice deep belly breathing for 5 minutes before meals to activate your parasympathetic nervous system.
Chew each bite 20-30 times — digestion begins in the mouth. Your Spleen will thank you.
Avoid iced drinks with meals. Cold liquids dampen digestive fire. Opt for warm tea or room temperature water.
Take a 10-minute walk after lunch to support blood sugar balance and energy flow.
Eat dinner at least 3 hours before bed to allow proper digestion before sleep.

dashboard.title: Your Personalized Wellness Dashboard

breakfast.tip: Start your day with warm, easily digestible foods

lunch.tip: Include a protein, complex carb, and cooked vegetables

dinner.tip: Eat dinner early and keep it light for better sleep

learn.title: Learn TCM Wellness

articles.title: Understanding the 9 Body Types in TCM
articles.summary: An overview of the nine constitution types and what they mean for your health.
articles.detail: The 9 TCM body constitutions (九种体质) are a foundational concept in Traditional Chinese Medicine. Each type represents a unique pattern of physical and energetic imbalance. The Balanced type (平和质) is the ideal — harmonious Yin and Yang. The other 8 types are imbalanced in different ways: Qi Deficiency (low energy), Yang Deficiency (cold), Yin Deficiency (heat), Phlegm-Dampness (stagnation), Damp-Heat (sticky heat), Blood Stasis (poor circulation), Qi Stagnation (emotional blockage), and Intrinsic (allergy-prone). Your quiz result identifies your unique combination.

articles.title: 气虚质 (Qi Deficiency) — When Your Battery is Low
articles.summary: Signs, causes, and dietary strategies for Qi deficiency.
articles.detail: Qi Deficiency is one of the most common TCM patterns in modern life. Think of Qi as your body's battery — when it's low, everything feels harder. Symptoms include fatigue, weak voice, spontaneous sweating, and a pale complexion. The Spleen (消化系统) is the organ most involved in Qi production. If your digestion is weak, your Qi production suffers. Key support: warm, cooked foods (soups, congee), avoiding raw/cold foods, and gentle exercise like Tai Chi. Ginger, dates, and astragalus (黄芪) are your best friends.

articles.title: 阳虚质 (Yang Deficiency) — The Cold Body Type
articles.summary: Why you feel cold all the time and how to warm up from within.
articles.detail: Yang Deficiency is like having a fireplace that doesn't burn hot enough. The key symptom is feeling cold — especially in the hands, feet, and lower back. You crave warmth and hot drinks. Your digestion tends to be slow, and you may have loose stools. The Kidneys (肾) are the source of Yang energy in TCM. Supporting Kidney Yang is the treatment principle. Eat warming foods: lamb, leeks, ginger, cinnamon, and walnuts. Avoid raw foods, iced drinks, and cold salads. Regular foot soaks in warm water can work wonders.

articles.title: 阴虚质 (Yin Deficiency) — The Heat Inside
articles.summary: Understanding internal heat and how to cool and nourish your body.
articles.detail: Yin Deficiency creates a sensation of internal heat — like a car engine running without enough coolant. You feel warm, especially in the palms and soles. Your throat and skin feel dry. You may experience night sweats and a thin, rapid pulse. Sleep is often disturbed, with vivid dreams. The Kidneys and Liver (肝肾) Yin need nourishment. Eat cooling, moistening foods: pears, lily bulb, white fungus, duck, and tofu. Avoid spicy foods, alcohol, and excessive exercise that causes sweating. Go to bed before 11 PM — nighttime is Yin time.

articles.title: ☀️ Damp-Heat (湿热质) — The Sticky Heat Type
articles.summary: Why you feel hot, heavy, and irritable — and how to cool down and dry out from within.
articles.detail: Damp-Heat (湿热质) is one of the most recognizable TCM constitution types. It's a combination of Dampness (fluid stagnation) and Heat (inflammation) in the body. Key signs: oily skin with persistent acne, a bitter taste in the mouth, sticky stools that are hard to flush, irritability, and a feeling of being weighed down. It affects approximately 10-15% of the population and is increasingly common in modern urban environments due to dietary habits (fried foods, alcohol, late-night eating) and lifestyle factors. The dietary strategy is to eat cooling, drying foods: bitter gourd, mung beans, coix seed, adzuki beans, celery, and green tea. Avoid: greasy foods, alcohol, spicy dishes, sugar, and late-night meals. Exercise that induces sweating (running, HIIT, swimming) helps drain Dampness. A warm, well-ventilated sleeping environment is essential. Read our full guide: Damp-Heat Body Type — Complete Guide.

articles.title: Brain Fog: A TCM Perspective
articles.summary: TCM views brain fog as a Spleen Qi deficiency. Learn how diet affects mental clarity.
articles.detail: In TCM, brain fog (脑雾) is typically a Spleen Qi Deficiency pattern. The Spleen is responsible for transforming food into clear energy (清气). When the Spleen is weak, the head feels heavy, cloudy, and unfocused. Dampness (湿) often accompanies this — a sticky, heavy sensation that clouds the mind. Common triggers: too many raw foods, dairy, sugar, and overthinking. Solutions: eat cooked, warming meals (breakfast congee with ginger), reduce screen time, and practice mindful movement. Ginseng (人参) and astragalus (黄芪) tea can help lift mental clarity.

articles.title: Afternoon Fatigue & The Body Clock
articles.summary: Why you crash at 2 PM according to the TCM organ clock.
articles.detail: In TCM's 24-hour organ clock (子午流注), the afternoon slump (1-3 PM) corresponds to the Small Intestine meridian (小肠经). This is when the body processes and absorbs nutrients from lunch. If your Spleen Qi is weak, you won't effectively extract energy from food, leading to a blood sugar crash. The 3-5 PM window (Bladder meridian) is another low-energy period for Kidney Qi deficient types. Solutions: eat a protein-rich, warm lunch (not just carbs), take a short walk after eating, and avoid iced drinks. A 15-minute power nap before 3 PM aligns with natural energy rhythms.

articles.title: Understanding Bloating from a TCM View
articles.summary: Bloating is often Spleen Qi stagnation. Discover which foods help.
articles.detail: In TCM, bloating (腹胀) is primarily a Spleen and Stomach (脾胃) dysfunction. The Spleen's job is to transform and transport (运化) — when it's weak, food stagnates as gas and distention. The most common bloating type is Spleen Qi Deficiency with Dampness (脾虚湿盛). Key triggers: eating while stressed, overeating, cold/raw foods, dairy, and excessive sweets. Emotional factors — worry and overthinking (思) directly weaken the Spleen according to the Five Element theory. Solutions: eat smaller portions, chew thoroughly, avoid drinking while eating, and use digestive herbs like cardamom (砂仁) and ginger.

articles.title: Insomnia: Finding Your Sleep Type
articles.summary: Different sleep issues point to different organ imbalances in TCM.
articles.detail: TCM identifies several insomnia (失眠) patterns, each needing different treatment. Heart Blood Deficiency (心血虚): difficulty falling asleep, mind racing. Liver Fire Blazing (肝火旺): waking between 1-3 AM, irritability. Yin deficiency: waking with night sweats, dry throat. Phlegm-heat: restless sleep, dreams, heavy snoring. The treatment depends entirely on your pattern. General tips: go to bed by 11 PM (Gallbladder meridian time), avoid screens 1 hour before bed, soak feet in warm water, and practice abdominal breathing. Lavender or sandalwood essential oils can calm the Shen (神).

articles.title: TCM vs. Western Nutrition: A Comparison
articles.summary: How two medical paradigms approach food and health differently.
articles.detail: Western nutrition breaks food down into chemical components: calories, protein, fat, carbohydrates, vitamins, and minerals. It asks "what is in this food?" TCM food therapy asks "what does this food DO in the body?" — its thermal nature (hot/warm/cool/cold), its flavor (sweet/sour/bitter/pungent/salty), and which organs it targets (meridian affinity). Both systems have value. Western nutrition excels at acute care and measurable deficiencies. TCM excels at preventive, personalized dietary guidance. The most powerful approach integrates both — using TCM's constitutional framework as the foundation, while acknowledging modern nutritional science for specifics.

articles.title: The Spleen in TCM vs. Western Medicine
articles.summary: The TCM Spleen is not the same organ — here's what it really means.
articles.detail: The TCM Spleen (脾) is very different from the anatomical pancreas/spleen of Western medicine. In TCM, the Spleen is a functional system responsible for transforming food into Qi and Blood, holding organs in place, and keeping blood in the vessels. Its partner organ is the Stomach (胃). The Spleen likes warmth and dislikes dampness. When weak, symptoms include fatigue, bloating, loose stools, and a feeling of heaviness. In Western medicine, the pancreas produces digestive enzymes and insulin; the spleen filters blood. The conditions are different, but the overlap — digestive health and energy production — is where TCM dietary therapy has its greatest strengths.

articles.title: Inflammation: A TCM and Western View
articles.summary: Western anti-inflammatory meets TCM heat-clearing approaches.
articles.detail: Western medicine sees inflammation as the immune system's response to injury or infection — acute (helpful) or chronic (harmful). TCM sees inflammation patterns through the lens of Heat (热): Excess Heat (实热) corresponds to acute inflammation with redness, swelling, and pain. Deficient Heat (虚热) corresponds to low-grade, chronic inflammation with dry heat sensations. Damp-Heat (湿热) — sticky, chronic inflammation with discharge (common in skin and digestive issues). Dietary approaches overlap significantly: both systems recommend anti-inflammatory foods like omega-3 rich fish, leafy greens, berries, and turmeric. TCM adds constitutional specificity — what reduces heat in one person may not work for another.

articles.title: Gut Health: One Concept, Two Paradigms
articles.summary: How TCM Spleen-Stomach theory aligns with modern microbiome research.
articles.detail: Modern gut health focuses on the microbiome — the trillions of bacteria living in your digestive tract. TCM's concept of Spleen-Stomach function (脾胃功能) covers remarkably similar ground but from a different angle. The Spleen's "transform and transport" (运化) function parallels the microbiome's role in breaking down food and producing nutrients. TCM's "Dampness" (湿) patterns often correspond to gut dysbiosis — the feeling of stagnation, bloating, and sticky stools. Both paradigms agree: whole foods, fermented foods, limited sugar, and stress management are foundational. TCM adds the insight that your specific gut issues relate to your constitutional type.

categories.title: Food & Herbs
categories.subtitle: 药食同源 — TCM ingredients explained

articles.title: Goji Berries (枸杞): TCM's Ancient Superfood
articles.summary: How 2,000 years of TCM use and modern science align on goji berry benefits.
articles.detail: Goji berries (枸杞 / Gǒu Qǐ) are one of the most scientifically validated herbs in TCM. Used for over 2,000 years to nourish Liver and Kidney Yin, brighten vision, and support longevity. Modern research confirms: they are rich in Lycium barbarum polysaccharides (LBPs) — powerful antioxidants — and zeaxanthin, which protects the macula of the eye. Studies show goji improves immune function, supports metabolic health, and may have neuroprotective effects. The classic TCM formula Qi Ju Di Huang Wan (杞菊地黄丸) combines goji with chrysanthemum for vision support. Daily dosage: 10-20g dried berries. Best consumed steeped in hot water, in soups, or added to congee. Avoid during acute infections. Read our full guide: Goji Berries in TCM.
